coredata.dc:title: One-step green synthesis of in–situ functionalized carbon quantum dots from Tagetes patula flowers: Applications as a fluorescent probe for detecting Fe3+ ions and as an antifungal agent
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

coredata.dc:description: In this research, for the first time, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized by using a green, simple, and efficient one-step hydrothermal carbonization technique from Tagetes patula flowers as carbon precursors without using any toxic chemicals are reported. The as-prepared CQDs have been characterized by Transmission electron microscope (TEM), Field emission scanning electron microscopy with Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (FESEM-EDX), UV–Visible spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray Diffraction (XRD), zeta potential analysis, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), fluorescence spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As analyzed by TEM and XRD analysis, it is observed that the as-synthesized CQDs are spherical having an average particle diameter of about 5.15 nm. The quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs was calculated and found to be 29.88%. The as-synthesized CQDs show an excellent fluorescence quenching behavior towards Fe3+ ions as compared to other metal ions. Therefore, CQDs were used as a sensing probe for detecting Fe3+ ions in a linear range of 0–4 μM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.32 μM. A comprehensive study has been conducted to show the effectiveness of the CQDs on the growth of rice plants (Oryza sativa) and the antifungal activities of CQDs on the mycelial growth of Magnaporthe oryzae (a plant pathogenic fungus).

(a) TEM image, (b) Particle size distribution histogram, (c) FESEM image, and (d) EDX spectrum of the CQDs.
(a) XRD and (b) Raman spectroscopy of the CQDs.
(a) Zeta potential distribution, (b) FTIR spectroscopy, (c) Wide scan XPS survey, (d) C1s spectrum, and (e) O1s spectrum of the CQDs.
(a) UV–Visible, (b) Fluorescence emission spectroscopy, (c) Excitation-wavelength-dependent emission spectra from 300 to 400 nm, (d) Quantum yield calculation plot of the CQDs.

(a) The effect of different pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs, (b) Histogram representation of the effect of different pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs (blue bar) and the fluorescence intensity changes observed after the addition of Fe3+ ions (100 μM concentrations) in the CQDs solution at different pHs.

(a) The selectivity of the fluorescence response of the CQDs in the absence and presence of various heavy metal ions (100 μM concentrations), (b) The fluorescence intensity ratio of the CQDs solutions containing different metal ions.
Selective fluorescence response of CQDs dispersions towards Fe3+ ions (100 μM concentration) (red bars), and interference of other metal ions (100 μM concentration) with Fe3+ ions (green bars).
(a) Fluorescence spectra of the sensor system in the presence of a different concentration of Fe3+ ions from 1 to 4 µM, (b) Calibration graph for the determination of the Fe3+ ion.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs solution at the time of synthesis and after 90 days of storage. (b) The fluorescence emission spectrum of CQD before and after UV lamp irradiation at 365 nm for 90 min.
Possible mechanism for fluorescence quenching of CQDs by Fe3+ ions.
Antifungal properties of CQDs at different concentrations (μg/mL) against the plant pathogenic fungus M. oryzae.

Fluorescence microscopic view of the root of the rice plant growth at different concentrations (μg/mL) respectively.
Comparison of CQDs-based sensors for Fe3+ detection.
Determination of Fe3+ in real samples with the proposed method.

head.ce:title: One-step green synthesis of in–situ functionalized carbon quantum dots from Tagetes patula flowers: Applications as a fluorescent probe for detecting Fe3+ ions and as an antifungal agent

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, for the first time, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) synthesized by using a green, simple, and efficient one-step hydrothermal carbonization technique from Tagetes patula flowers as carbon precursors without using any toxic chemicals are reported. The as-prepared CQDs have been characterized by Transmission electron microscope (TEM), Field emission scanning electron microscopy with Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (FESEM-EDX), UV–Visible spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray Diffraction (XRD), zeta potential analysis, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), fluorescence spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As analyzed by TEM and XRD analysis, it is observed that the as-synthesized CQDs are spherical having an average particle diameter of about 5.15 nm. The quantum yield (QY) of the CQDs was calculated and found to be 29.88%. The as-synthesized CQDs show an excellent fluorescence quenching behavior towards Fe3+ ions as compared to other metal ions. Therefore, CQDs were used as a sensing probe for detecting Fe3+ ions in a linear range of 0–4 μM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.32 μM. A comprehensive study has been conducted to show the effectiveness of the CQDs on the growth of rice plants (Oryza sativa) and the antifungal activities of CQDs on the mycelial growth of Magnaporthe oryzae (a plant pathogenic fungus).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, a novel class of carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), having diameters less than 10 nm has attracted a great deal of interest from the scientific community [1]. CQDs were initially unexpectedly revealed in 2004, while single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) were being purified [2,3]. Fluorescent CQDs possess many unique chemical and physical properties such as high water solubility, stable fluorescence behavior, low toxicity, chemical inertness, low cost, and excellent biocompatibility, which makes them more attractive in comparison to conventional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [4–7]. Because of their outstanding physicochemical properties, CQDs have a lot of potential applications, which include cell imaging, photocatalysis, biological labelling, biosensors, chemical sensors, drug delivery, fluorescent inks, solar cells, different optoelectronic device application, medical diagnosis, and multiple applications in analytical chemistry such as fluorescence probes for the sensing of inorganic molecules [8–13]. However, the physiochemical properties and multimodal applications of CQDs depend on the nature of carbon resource materials used to prepare CQDs and their preparation method. Several synthetic techniques have been proposed to synthesize CQDs such as chemical oxidation [14], acidic oxidations [15], microwave/ultrasonic passivation [16], arc discharge [17], pyrolysis [18], electrochemical synthesis [19], laser ablation [20], hydrothermal carbonization method [21–23], plasma treatment [24], and microwave-assisted synthesis [25]. Recently, the carbonization of various organic molecules has been used to synthesize CQDs, such as citric acid, sucrose, glycol, ascorbic acid, etc. [26–29]. However, the aforementioned methods to synthesize CQDs with high quantum yield (QY) have several limitations, such as the use of various toxic chemicals as carbon precursors, use of toxic solvents, smaller range spectral efficiency, low product yields, complicated post-treatment processes, etc. [30,31]. Using these toxic chemical precursors and solvents in the synthesis of CQDs does not fulfil the principles of sustainable and green chemistry and is hazardous to both people and the environment [32,33]. Therefore, nowadays, researchers are looking for simple, clean, cost-effective, repeatable, and environmentally friendly green synthetic methods for synthesizing CQDs by using various renewable resources as green precursors since it is readily available in nature and nontoxic solvents. Among the reported methods, the hydrothermal carbonization technique is regarded as a green synthetic strategy as it is convenient, efficient, uses water as a solvent, and produces CQDs with a high QY. So far, to develop biomass-derived CQDs, various natural renewable green raw materials such as hair [34], rice husk [35], prawn shells [31], lychee seeds [36], orange peels [37], peanut skin [38], peanut shells [39], milk [40], banana plant [41], orange juice [21], sweet potato [42], coffee grounds [43], watermelon peel [44], bamboo leaves [45], capsicum [46], onion waste [47], and leaves of tulsi plant [48] already being used as natural green precursors for the synthesis of fluorescent CQDs. Biomass-derived CQDs made from these natural sources have tunable functional groups on the surface due to the presence of various components, which show excellent fluorescence and sensing properties, which lead to enhanced sensitivity for target molecules. Therefore, being motivated, we have adopted green synthetic technique routes for the synthesis of CQDs by using renewable green raw materials as precursors.

Among the various heavy metal ions, one of the more readily available transition metals is the ferric ion (Fe3+), which causes a major threat to human health, plants, animals, and the environment, have been a matter of relentless efforts in recent years because of their high stability in the ecosystem [49,50]. Fe3+ ions play a vital role in various biological mechanisms, such as DNA and RNA synthesis, oxygen metabolism, haemoglobin’s oxygen-carrying capacity, and in environmental systems [51,52]. As a result, for the surveillance of environmental and human health, Fe3+ ions detection is a crucial task for the scientists, which captivated a lot of interest worldwide. To date, several traditional techniques have been established for the sensing of heavy metal ions, which include atomic fluorescence spectrometry [53], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [54], atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [55], potentiometric sensors [56], and electrochemical based methods [57]. However, these conventional techniques possess some drawbacks, such as requiring very sophisticated instruments with complex techniques, high costs, and longer processing times. Therefore, nowadays, fluorescence assay-based sensors have received more attention as the best tool for the detection of heavy metal ions because of their low interference, simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and quick response. A few works of literature have been reported based on sensing Fe3+ ions via fluorescent CQDs [58,59]. Atchudan et al. [60] group synthesized nitrogen-doped CQDs by using phyllanthus acidus as a precursor, which can be used as a fluorescent sensing probe for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions. Zhao et al. [61] produced fluorescent CQDs using pine wood as raw materials and employed it as a fluorescent sensing probe to detect Fe3+ ions. Latief et al. [62] synthesized green fluorescent nitrogen-rich CQDs using gelatin as a precursor by hydrothermal approach, and they used it as a probe for the sensing of Fe3+ ions.

In the present research study, we have established an eco-friendly and green synthetic strategy for the synthesis of fluorescent CQDs by using flowers of Tagetes patula as green precursors through the simple and cost-effective one-step hydrothermal carbonization technique without using any toxic chemicals and surface passivating agents. The flowers of Tagetes patula, also named marigold flowers in India, are a potential ornamental plant that was used in ancient times for the treatment of fever, diarrhea, skin, vomiting, hepatic, and colic diseases. The flowers of Tagetes patula contain terpenoids, thiophenes, flavonoids, alkaloids, carotenoids, phenolic compounds, etc., which can be used in various biological pharmacological activities such as antifungal, nematicidal, anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antibacterial [63,64]. Since flowers are usually thrown as bio-waste and have no economic value, therefore, we have utilized these flowers as a green precursor for the synthesis of CQDs without using any toxic solvents. The prepared CQDs were characterized by Field emission scanning electron microscopy with Energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (FESEM-EDX), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–visible absorption (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), zeta potential measurements, Raman spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy techniques. Since CQDs synthesized from the flowers of Tagetes patula as green precursors have the ability to detect metal ions, therefore for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions, CQDs were employed as a fluorescent probe. It is interesting to note that the synthesized CQDs demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ ions in the linear range of 1 μM to 4 μM, in contrast to other metal ions (Zn2+, Sn2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, K+, Cu2+, and Co2+). It was discovered that the Fe3+ ions limit of detection (LOD) was 0.32 μM, which was significantly lower than the earlier published research (synthesized using green precursors) [65,61]. The CQDs were also used to detect Fe3+ in actual water samples, with a recovery rate of 98.9–106.4%, demonstrating the excellent analytical efficiency of this CQD-based sensor. Furthermore, the prepared CQDs have been used as a model candidate to investigate the impact of it on the growth of the rice plant (Oryza sativa). Moreover, we have investigated the antifungal properties of CQDs against Magnaporthe oryzae, a plant pathogenic fungus that causes the devastating rice blast disease in rice plants [66].

Flowers of Tagetes patula (French marigold) were collected from Rourkela, Odisha, India. All the chemicals such as MnCl2, Zn(NO3)2, NiCl2, MgCl2, FeCl3, KNO3, CdCl2, CuCl2, SnCl2, and Co(NO3)2 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. Chemical reagents used throughout the experimental procedure are analytical grade and used without performing additional purification. Throughout the experiment, all the aqueous solutions were prepared with Milli-Q water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from Tagetes patula flowers
ce:section.ce:para: Flowers of Tagetes patula were used as precursors in the synthesis of CQDs by using a simple one-step hydrothermal carbonization technique. The flower petals were broken down into smaller pieces, dried outside in the sun, rinsed thoroughly with a mixture of Milli-Q water, acetone, and ethanol, and then dried for another 10 h in the oven at 80 °C. Firstly, 70 mL of Milli-Q water was taken in a beaker, then 4 g of dried flowers were mixed with it, and then it was ultra-sonicated for 30 min till the change in water color from white to burnt orange. After that, the mixture was moved from the beaker to an autoclave lined with Teflon and heated for 5 h at 220 °C. The yellowish-orange solution was collected once it reached to room temperature, and then the unreacted organic moieties and large particles were extracted by centrifuging it for 20 min at 12,000 rpm. Then, fluorescent CQDs were obtained after filtration through a 0.22 μm syringe filter. Finally, obtained highly purified fluorescent CQDs were kept in a freezer at 4 °C for further characterization and experiments. The schematic depiction for the synthesis of CQDs from the flowers of Tagetes patula is shown in Fig. 1 .

A UV–Visible Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu/UV2450) was used to record the UV–Visible absorption spectra of the prepared CQDs. The range of the wavelength for recording the absorbance spectra of CQDs was kept between 200 nm and 800 nm. The fluorescence spectra of the synthesized CQDs were obtained using a Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Jobin Yvon Fluoromax-4P). A Fourier-transform infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR, Shimadzu IR Prestige-21) was used to study the existence of various functional groups on the surface of the as-synthesized CQDs using the KBr pellet technique. Raman spectra were determined by using a Raman spectrometer designed with a solid-state laser having a wavelength of 633 nm with a 50 mW power scan range of 400–3000 /cm (WITec Wissenschaftliche Instrumente und Technologie GmbH Model-XMB3000-3000) to study the intramolecular bonds present in the synthesized CQDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a multipurpose X-ray Diffractometer (RIGAKU, JAPAN & ULTIMA -IV) with 2θ ranges from 20˚ to 80˚ to study the crystallinity of CQDs. A transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI, Tecnai G2 TF30-ST) was used to analyze the morphology of the prepared CQDs. The surface functional groups and chemical composition of the as-prepared CQDs were studied using the X-ray Photo-Electron Spectroscopy (XPS) Beamline (BL-14) under ultrahigh vacuum conditions at the Indus-2 synchrotron in RRCAT Indore, India. The zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, NanoZS90) instrument was used to measure the zeta potential. 1 mL of well-dispersed CQDs sample in water was taken in a transparent reusable zeta cell for the measurement. Three sets of measurements were taken at room temperature to estimate the value of zeta potential.

To study the selectivity for various metal ions, in a standard process, various metal salts cation such as Cu2+, Co2+, K+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Sn2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, and Zn2+ stock solutions were prepared for concentrations of 100 μM using Milli-Q water. The maximum fluorescence intensity was recorded as F0 by taking 2.5 mL of 10-fold diluted CQDs in a quartz cuvette (Blank). Following the addition of each metal ion, the CQDs solution was stirred as normally. After incubating it for 5 min, the fluorescence spectra at excitation wavelength of 360 nm were then recorded. All the experimental procedures were performed at room temperature. The selectivity of the as-prepared CQDs for Fe3+ metal ions was determined using the measured fluorescence spectrum. Moreover, the Limit of Detection (LOD) experiment for highly sensitive metal ions (Fe3+) was performed in triplicate at lower concentrations (1–4 µM), and the results obtained from this were fitted to the Stern–Volmer equation, F0/F = 1 + Ksv [X]. Here Ksv represents the slope of the linear fit, [X] represents the concentration of metal ion, and F0 and F represents the fluorescence intensities without and with metal ions, respectively.

To calculate the QY (ΦCQD) of the as-prepared CQDs, a previously defined approach was followed [67,68]. To reduce the effects of re-absorption, five different concentrations of the sample (CQDs) and reference (quinine sulfate) were made, each of which had an absorbance value less than 0.10 measured at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. To prepare the reference solution, quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M of H2SO4 (quantum yield 54%, index of refraction (ɳ) = 1.33). QY of the sample was then calculated by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensities recorded at the same excitation wavelength and absorbance value of the sample with the references. The QY (ΦCQD) of the as-synthesized CQDs was estimated by using the below equation:
Φ

Where Φ represents the quantum yield, the subscript CQD and R represent the sample and references respectively, A stands for the absorbance, I represent the integrated fluorescence emission intensity, m is the slope, and η is the refractive index of the solvent medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Seed sterilization and rice plant growth
ce:section.ce:para: Rice plant growth was studied under sterile conditions. Before rice seed germination, sterilization of seeds was done. For sterilization, rice seeds were pre-washed with a solution of a mixture of 0.05% bavistin and 70% EtOH for 1 min, and further, it was placed in 1.2% sodium hypochlorite for 10 min. Then the seed was washed three to four times with sterile water, and then finally, it was washed with distilled water, and then it was transferred into glass tubes for further experiment. To study the effect of CQDs on the rice plant growth conditions, sterilized rice seeds taken in six different glass tubes were soaked in water with CQDs solution distributed on it to varying concentrations of 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, and 1.00 μg/mL respectively, and left it for 10 days for the growth. The rice seeds soaked in water without CQDs were used as a control. After 10 days of rice plant growth, it was observed under the fluorescence microscope.

To study the antifungal activities of the prepared CQDs, prepared CQDs were tested against the plant pathogenic fungus Magnaporthe oryzae, which was grown in oatmeal agar for 7 days. For the inhibition assay, fresh spore inoculation was prepared by washing the fungal plate with sterile water (containing 0.01% tween 20). Then, the mycelial solution was mixed well by vortex and filtered through two-layer cheesecloth. Then, 2x10ʹ4 number spore was taken in 96 well petri dish along with 2x PDB growth media containing streptomycin and CQDs solution at concentrations of 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, and 1.00 μg/mL respectively. Media without CQDs is taken as control. After incubation for 24 h, the mycelial growth inhibition of pathogenic fungus was observed in each petri dish under the microscope.

The surface morphology, average particle size, and elemental composition of as-synthesized CQDs were examined by TEM and FESEM. As depicted in Fig. 2 (a) and (c), CQDs revealed that they have uniform dispersion and all of them are spherical in the range from 3 to 8 nm. From the TEM image, it is also revealed that their CQDs are small crystallinity in nature. The particle size distribution histogram of the as-synthesized CQDs obtained from several hundred particles is shown in Fig. 2 (b), and the average diameter was estimated to be around 5.15 nm, which is comparable with the previously reported literature [69,70,71]. The EDX spectrum (Fig. 2 (d)) of the synthesized CQDs revealed that the sample contains 73.53 wt% of carbon.

The XRD spectra of the as-prepared CQDs revealed two broad characteristic peaks, as shown in Fig. 3 (a) one is at about 2θ = 26°, and the other is at about 43°, which is associated with the (002) and (100) lattice planes of the graphitic carbon (sp2 hybridization) respectively. The broad diffraction peak noticed in the XRD pattern owing to the densely packed crystalline carbon atoms having oxygen-containing groups [72].

Raman spectroscopy was studied to confirm the graphitic carbon. As shown in Fig. 3 (b), the Raman spectra of the CQDs exhibited two specific bands at around 1368 cm−1 and 1568 cm−1, which corresponded to the D band and the G band. The G band correlates to the sp2-conjugated structure of carbon, while the D band signifies the defects [73]. To determine the structural purity of the prepared CQDs towards graphite, the relative intensity ratio (ID/IG) of the disordered D band (ID) to the crystalline G band (IG) was estimated and found to be 0.91, indicating that the prepared CQDs has good quality and is mainly made up of graphitic carbon cluster [74].

The zeta potential (surface charge) of the as-prepared CQDs was investigated and found to be −21.9 mV, as shown in Fig. 4 (a). From this result, we confirmed that the prepared CQDs are negative in charge may be due to the presence of negative charges in oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl) on the surfaces of CQDs.

FTIR spectroscopy was recorded to investigate the functional groups present on the surface of the prepared CQDs, as shown in Fig. 4 (b). The O-H stretching vibration mode of the phenolic functional groups corresponds to a broad distinctive absorption band at 3431 cm−1 in the synthesized CQDs. The absorption peaks at 2922 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 can be attributed to the C-H and C = C stretching vibration modes respectively. The peaks at 1447 cm−1 can be ascribed to the deformation of C-H bending vibrations, and peaks at 1381 cm−1 correlate to the COO– symmetric stretching vibration mode. C-O and C-O-C stretching vibrations appear at the absorption bands of 1242 cm−1 and 1099 cm−1 respectively [75,76]. Additionally, the presence of C-H out-of-plane bending vibrations was revealed by the absorption bands at 887 cm−1. From these observations, it is concluded that the prepared CQDs are primarily made of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms with carboxyl, hydroxyl, methyl, and carboxylic functional groups present on the surfaces, which imparts excellent water solubility and stability for various biological applications. No further processing was carried out to functionalize the prepared CQDs.

For further confirmation of the chemical compositions and surface functional groups of the as-synthesized CQDs, XPS analysis was performed. From the XPS result, we confirmed that mainly carbon (C) and oxygen (O) are present in the CQDs. The wide scan XPS survey of CQDs as shown in Fig. 4 (c) revealed two peaks: the first one represents the C1s peak, and the second one represents the O1s peak. The high-resolution XPS scan of the C1s spectrum, as shown in Fig. 4 (d), revealed four distinct peaks that were ascribed to 284.89 eV (C-C), 286.33 eV (C-OH), 288.32 eV (C-O), and 288.95 eV (C = O) respectively. From the high-resolution XPS scan of O1s spectra, as shown in Fig. 4 (e), mainly two peaks are confirmed, which corresponded to 532.29 eV (C-O) and 534.24 eV (C = O) respectively [77].

The UV–Vis spectroscopy of the prepared CQDs exhibits three strong absorption peaks as illustrated in Fig. 5 (a): two of which are at around 248 nm and 273 nm owing to the π-π* electronic transition of the C = C bonds, and the third one is at 319 nm because of the n-π* electronic transition of the C = O aromatic rings [42].

The fluorescence properties of the as-prepared CQDs were studied by fluorescence spectroscopy as displayed in Fig. 5 (b). The representative fluorescence spectra show a strong bright blue fluorescence at 437 nm exhibited by CQDs when excited at 360 nm, but the CQDs solution was pale yellow color in daylight. The synthesized CQDs displayed an excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior as shown in Fig. 5 (c). It shows the different emission wavelengths at different wavelengths of excitation ranging from 300 to 380 nm with a continuous interval of 10 nm. As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission wavelength red-shifted. The fluorescence behavior of the CQDs can be explained by the giant red edge effect, aromatic conjugate structures, the emissive traps, and attached surface functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl [78].

The absorbance and integrated fluorescence intensities values measured for the sample (CQDs) and references (quinine sulfate) at the wavelength of excitation of 360 nm were plotted, and the slopes were calculated as shown in Fig. 5 (d). The measured data shows good linearity with an intercept value of approximately zero. The quantum yield of the as-prepared CQDs from the marigold flower was estimated using the obtained values and found to be 29.88%. From this result, it can be easily seen that the quantum yield of CQDs hydrothermally synthesized from marigold flowers has a quantum yield of 29.88%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH on the fluorescence of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensities of the CQDs was studied at the different pHs (3, 5, 7, 9, and 11), and the acquired results are shown in Fig. 6 (a). The PH value of the as-synthesized CQDs solutions was calibrated from pH 7 to acidic (pH 3 and 5) and basic (pH 9 and 11) as needed by using the 1 M hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide solution. The measured pH value of the CQDs solution is ascribed as 7, and at this value highest fluorescence intensity was observed. We have taken pH 7 as the optimum pH throughout the whole experiment. When the pH of the CQDs solution was adjusted to a basic or acidic solution, then we observed that there is a small decrease in the fluorescence intensity. The reason for these changes in the fluorescence intensity may be due to the remarkable changes in the functional groups present on the surfaces induced by H+ or OH– ions. This type of feature might be related to the protonation and deprotonation nature of the functional groups present on the surfaces of the CQDs [79]. Then, using CQDs at various pHs (3, 5, 7, 9 and 11), we further examined the fluorescence intensity of Fe3+ metal ions (at a concentration of 100 μM) as shown in Fig. 6 (b). In comparison to the other pH levels, pH 7 had the greatest levels of PL quenching and was the best pH level for the detection of Fe3+ metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of Fe3+ ions through fluorescence response of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the selectivity properties of the CQDs, the fluorescence spectrum of CQDs added with each metal ion (100 μM concentrations) was measured at the exciting wavelength of 360 nm. From the fluorescence spectrum, as shown in Fig. 7 (a), different degrees of quenching and shifts in the intensity of fluorescence of the CQDs for different metal ions were detected. It is also revealed from the spectrum that CQDs with Fe3+ ions have lower fluorescence emission intensity as compared to other metal ions, indicating that there is some form of exchange of energy among Fe3+ ions and CQDs.

From the histogram plot, as illustrated in Fig. 7 (b), it is noticed that there are remarkable changes in the F/F0 values in the absence and presence of various heavy metal ions in the CQDs solution, with Fe3+ ions exhibiting the minimum value of F/F0. From this, we confirmed that, in comparison to other metal ions, Fe3+ ions strongly quenched the fluorescence of the CQDs, which is comparable to the previously reported literatures [80,81].

To further evaluate the selectivity of the CQDs towards Fe3+ ions, the effect of different interfering metal ions (100 μM concentrations), such as Cd2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Zn2+ on the fluorescent response of the CQDs sensing probe were examined along with Fe3+ (100 μM concentration) as shown in Fig. 8 . The results revealed that the existence of interfering metal ions had no noticeable impact on the sensing of Fe3+ ions.

The fluorescence quenching efficiency of real sample analysis was examined to further confirm the high sensitivity of the as-synthesized CQDs towards the Fe3+ ions concentrations. The different concentrations of Fe3+ ions ranging from 1 to 4 μM were used to prepare spiked samples. With increasing the concentrations of the Fe3+ ions in the sample, the intensity of the fluorescence emission of the CQDs is considerably decreasing as shown in Fig. 9 (a) recorded at the exciting wavelength of 360 nm. The Stern–Volmer equation, F0/F = 1 + Ksv [X] plot as shown in Fig. 9 (b), is used to examine the quenching mechanism of fluorescence. Here, F0 and F stands for the intensity of fluorescence emission of the CQDs solution without and with Fe3+ ions respectively, X is the Fe3+ ion concentrations (range from 1 to 4 μM) dissolved in the CQDs solution, and Ksv is the linear fit slope or quenching constant. We inferred from this plot that, F0/F and the concentrations of Fe3+ ions had a linear relationship. Consequently, it is observed that there is a strong linear correlation between the Fe3+ ions concentrations and the quenching efficiency with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.99884 and Ksv = 0.01045. The LOD was estimated to be 0.32 μM by using the following equation:
L i m i t
o f
d e t e c t i o n
L O D
=
3 σ
m

Where m is the linear fit slope and σ is the standard deviation of the CQDs blank signal. From the overall result, it is concluded that the as-synthesized CQDs can be widely applied in the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions by using this methodology. Noticeably, the measured LOD values for Fe3+ ions from this experiment are prominently lowered than the World Health Organization (WHO) guidelines value (300 μM). Furthermore, we compared the linear range and limit of detection of our approach with previously reported CQDs-based Fe3+ sensors (see Table 1 ). From the findings in Table 1, it is clear that our method has a number of benefits over the other methods, including a lower LOD and a broad linear range. Additionally, the interaction between the CQDs and Fe3+ ions is very quick to quench the fluorescence intensity, and it can be finished in less than 60 s.

The stability and photostability of the CQDs was further investigated which is one of the important properties for practical applications. The fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs solution at various storage durations are shown in Fig. 10 (a). At the beginning of the synthesis and on the 90th day of preservation, the fluorescence intensities of CQDs are nearly identical. These negligible intensity variations confirms the prolongevity of CQDs. Fig. 10 (b) shows the results of examining CQDs’ photostability before and after exposure to 365 nm radiation using a UV lamp for 90 min. Notably, irradiated CQDs exhibit only small differences in the fluorescence intensity from non-irradiated CQDs, which confirms the excellent photostability of CQDs [88].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To exploit the practical applicability of as-prepared CQDs sensors, the standard addition technique was used on real water samples obtained from the tap water in our laboratory. The tap water was pre-treated before use to minimize errors in the experiment. After being heated to remove any remaining chlorine, a 0.45 μm membrane filter was used to filter the tap water sample. A recovery experiment was carried out in the water sample after being spiked with a standard concentration of Fe3+ (5, 10, 20 μM) because no iron was detected in the tap water. The corresponding recovery (%) for these samples ranges from 98.90% to 106.40% are shown in Table 2 . To determine the relative standard deviation, the experiment was run repeatedly three times under similar experimental conditions. The findings demonstrate excellent agreement with earlier reported studies [89], and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than 1% for all the observations. This validates the high efficiency and reliability of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism for fluorescence quenching of CQDs by Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the foregoing characterizations, it was confirmed that the prepared CQDs are negative surface charge and abundant in functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surfaces of the CQDs. Due to the negatively charged CQDs and vibrant functional groups present on the surfaces of CQDs, there is a formation of metal complex through the strong coordination bond between Fe3+ ions and the CQDs. As a result, there is a decrease in the fluorescence emission intensity when Fe3+ ions interact with the CQDs.

This finding can be ascribed to the potential of Fe3+ ions for quenching of the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs, instead of chelating with the CQDs surfaces most probably via electron energy transfer between CQDs and Fe3+ ions [90]. The outer electronic configuration of Fe3+ ions was a 3d5 system and all these five d orbitals were half-filled. In order to complete their half-filled 3d orbitals, they are more tending to accept the electrons present in the excited state of the CQDs (due to the coordination interaction) and result in nonradiative electron/hole recombination. The resultant fluorescence quenching was may be due to this mechanism [91]. The schematic representation of the plausible mechanism for fluorescence quenching of CQDs by Fe3+ ions is shown in Fig. 11 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifungal properties of the CQDs and rice plant growth
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the antifungal activities of CQDs on the mycelial growth of the plant pathogenic fungus Magnaporthe oryzae, first of all, the fungus was prepared and then incubated in the petri dish with media containing CQDs at different concentrations of 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, and 1.00 μg/mL respectively for 24 h. Hyphae is one of the structures of fungi which can penetrate the plant tissues and the vascular system to absorb nutrients from it, causing plant infection which can lead to rice blast disease. As shown in Fig. 12 , it is clearly visible that before treating the fungus with CQDs, more branching of fungal hyphae is observed and after treating it with CQDs, the branching of fungal hyphae decreased with increasing the concentration of CQDs from 0.02 to 1.00 μg/mL in the difference of 0.02 respectively. We conclude from this observation that CQDs can effectively inhibit the mycelial growth rate of the fungus by reducing the branching of fungal hyphae with an increasing the concentration of CQDs. However, the exact mechanism of the influence of CQDs on the branching of fungal hyphae is still unknown and needs detailed study.

For investigation of the effectiveness of the CQDs on the rice plant (Oryza sativa) growth, sterilized rice seed was soaked in water for seed germination. Then different concentrations of CQDs solution were distributed on it. After 10 days of rice plant growth, accumulation of CQDs on rice plants was observed under the fluorescence microscope. Since CQDs possess a strong fluorescence, their accumulation in various parts of the plant can be noticeable by their fluorescence signal. As shown in Fig. 13 , no fluorescence signal is detected under visible light, but a strong blue fluorescence signal is observed in the root rather than on the leaf, stem, and any other part of the rice plant under UV light. No fluorescence signal is detected from the control rice plant growth as there is no accumulation of CQDs in the root of the rice plant. However, when we increase the concentrations of CQDs from 0.02 to 1.00 μg/mL in the difference of 0.02, an increase in the accumulation of the CQDs in the root of the rice plant is observed.

To further confirm this result, we observed rice plant growth under the fluorescence microscope. As shown in Fig. 14 , we observed that the CQDs could accumulate more in the root than in the other parts of the rice plant as we increase the concentration of CQDs. Then the accumulated CQDs in the root is successively spreading to the different parts of the plant and hence boosting the rice plant growth which is directly related to the oxygen contents of the CQDs. Water molecules can interact easily with the hydrophilic groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl groups) present on the CQDs surface which represents the oxygen content of it, therefore helping the molecules of water to penetrate more into the rice seed [92]. If the oxygen content of CQDs is more, then there is more accumulation of CQDs in the root of the rice plant hence boosting the growth of the plant.

Several antifungal drugs have already been discovered to inhibit the growth of the pathogenic fungus M. oryzae, such as fluconazole (FLC), caspofungin (CFG), and 5-flucytosine (5FC), and these exhibited antifungal activity at the minimum concentration of 32 μg/mL [93]. All these synthetic fungicides have now the leading cause of severe pollution and environmental degradation. To minimize this problem, numerous natural product-derived antifungal drugs have been reported. For example, citral derived from Litsea cubeba fruit demonstrated an outstanding antifungal effect towards the pathogenic fungus M. oryzae at 100 μg/mL concentration [94]. Carbendazim–graphene oxide (CBZ–GO) showed higher antifungal activity against M. oryzae at 0.28 μg/mL concentration than the conventional CBZ (0.64 μg/mL) [95]. Our newly synthesized CQDs, demonstrated antifungal effects at very low concentrations (0.08 μg/mL) as compared to previously reported drugs, which exhibited inhibitory activity against M. oryzae at higher concentrations. Therefore, CQDs could be used as a promising drug against the pathogenic fungus M. oryzae.

In this research work, a simple, inexpensive, environment-friendly, and green method have been adopted to prepare bright blue fluorescence CQDs by using Tagetes patula flowers as natural carbon precursors via one-pot hydrothermal carbonization techniques without using any surface passivating agents successfully. The TEM and XRD analysis revealed that all the synthesized CQDs are spherical with an average particle size of about 5.12 nm with poor crystallinity in nature. Different functional groups including carboxyl and hydroxyl groups present on the surfaces of the green synthesized CQDs are studied by FTIR and XPS spectroscopy techniques. The Raman spectroscopy identified the chemical bonds present in the CQDs. The synthesized CQDs showed excellent properties such as high solubility, strong fluorescence behavior, and excitation-dependent emission intensity properties. The green synthesized CQDs are negatively charged and have interesting pH-dependent behavior at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The QY of the synthesized CQDs was found to be 29.88%. Moreover, the synthesized CQDs were successfully employed as an excellent fluorescence sensing probe for sensing Fe3+ ions with a detection limit of 0.32 μM. Due to several advantages of the as-synthesized CQDs such as easy synthesis techniques, excellent fluorescence behavior, low cytotoxicity, and low cost, they can be widely used in the field of detection of Fe3+ ions in real environmental samples, optoelectronics, and biological applications. The results also indicate that the CQDs can effectively boost the growth of rice plants (Oryza sativa), and it also exhibits excellent antifungal property against the plant pathogenic fungus Magnaporthe oryzae. Hence, CQDs could be used as a promising candidate against the rice blast disease caused by the plant pathogenic fungus M. oryzae.
